description: To 'tukiro' is to reconsider a prior matter systematically under stated evidence or criteria. The review may leave the earlier decision in place or change what follows from it. 'whetuma' (appeal) is a request that review occur; the two acts need not have the same participant.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into high-back rounded 'u'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into stressed high-front 'i'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before final true-mid rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Dental 'tu' recalls the opening of 'turema' (return). The crisp centre and final tongue turn preserve 'kiro' (precise), so the word audibly returns to something through a narrower point of attention.
usage_notes: The reviewing person or body is the subject. A prior decision, record, procedure, or action becomes the object. The verb has ordinary noun use for a review. Participants and authority belong to the arrangement; exact review standards and deadlines remain with their sources. 'nilaki' (inspect) examines the condition of a system or object rather than reconsidering a prior matter.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Review lets an institution revisit resource decisions and infrastructure rules when conditions or evidence change.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A defined review path gives criticism somewhere to go after the first decision instead of reducing it to ceremonial listening.

semantic_domains.activity: systematically reconsidering an earlier decision, record, or action
semantic_domains.cognition: reassessment conducted under stated evidence or criteria
